danger_signals.meaning: Genetic or environmental mismatch; ecosystem collapse risk

danger_signals.visual_form: Reduced creature count visible in environment display
danger_signals.meaning: Species struggling to survive current conditions

danger_signals.visual_form: Predator population spikes or crashes relative to prey

safety_signals.visual_form: Consistent organism count maintained across generations
Multiple species coexisting and visible in world
Offspring generation visible; genetic traits propagating

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Barren or seed world with selected environment type (terrain, climate parameters displayed)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No organism movement; awaiting species introduction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Initial species placed; sparse organism distribution across world map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Creatures begin moving, feeding, reproducing at base genetic parameters

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Organism appearance changes subtly across generations; trait variations visible in population
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Species behavior adapts to selection pressures; feeding patterns, movement speed, reproductive rate fluctuate

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: World parameters shift (temperature, terrain, light levels); creature distress indicators possible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Species migration attempts; increased mortality; rapid evolution or extinction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ecosystem visually static with predictable organism positions and densities
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Consistent feeding, breeding, predation cycles; populations maintain steady state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: User-defined world topology, climate zones, resource distribution displayed distinctly
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Species performance metrics reflect environmental fit; survival rates update in real-time

environmental_storytelling.example: Creature appearance directly reflects genetic modification—wings, size, coloration match coded traits; visual phenotype communicates genotype
Predator-prey relationships visualized through spatial clustering and chase behaviors; food chain hierarchy evident in organism positioning
Desert vs. forest vs. arctic environments shown with distinct terrain/climate visuals; species survival or thriving indicates genetic fit to displayed conditions
Charts and statistics windows display population curves, genetic diversity metrics; visual data directly linked to visible organism behavior
Species genealogy accessible through UI; visual organisms linked to parent genetics; trait inheritance visible across generational display

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Slow, deliberate movement; active reproduction; stable population growth
Rapid, erratic movement; reduced breeding; population decline
Active hunting visible; high predation success; population increase
Increased search/roaming; reduced kill rate; starvation deaths visible
Rapid mortality; failed reproduction; visual decline in population density
Increased breeding success; stable or growing population; phenotypic shifts visible in offspring

notes: SimLife lacks traditional danger/faction systems. Instead, world-state communication relies on ecological visualization: organism survival, population curves, and genetic trait expression serve as primary feedback. The game's manifestation layer is data-driven—ecosystem health communicated through quantitative visual displays (population graphs, genetic statistics) rather than dramatic environmental cues. Exclusive creatures (Killer Penguin, Monkeyphant, Orgot) serve as design examples and visual markers of creative possibility rather than faction identifiers. Danger exists as abstract ecosystem failure (extinction cascades, unsustainable food chains) rather than explicit threats. Safety is communicated through stable, diverse, self-sustaining populations.
